Officially considered the same species,: Officially considered the same species, with a southeastern and Atlantic subspecies, as well as intermediate forms on the south coast (False Bay to Agulhas).
The hydroid provides protection in the f: The hydroid provides protection in the form of a living, growing home, as well as by covering the shell and offering defence through its stinging cells. The hermit crab, in turn, brings the benefits of a mobile lifestyle to an otherwise sessile organism.
The sponge provides a living, growing ho: The sponge provides a living, growing home and the hermit crab brings the benefits of a mobile lifestyle to an otherwise sessile organism.
Could potentially be confused with this: Could potentially be confused with this species in the field as both occur in the same habitat.
Similar colour and overall appearance, a: Similar colour and overall appearance, and overlap in habitat on soft sediments.
Limpet amphipods live in pairs inside th: Limpet amphipods live in pairs inside the mantle cavities of limpets.
Shore spider eggs are parasitised by a s: Shore spider eggs are parasitised by a small wasp.
Bamboo kelp provides the habitat for the: Bamboo kelp provides the habitat for the Kelp limpet - in return the Kelp limpets keep the kelp free from overgrowing epiphytes.
U-shaped burrows in the blade of the kel: U-shaped burrows in the blade of the kelp, amphipods are forming a colony inside and likely feeding on the kelp. Kelp plants can get structurally weakened.
frontend-text-legal_notice: Please note that you are viewing an unpublished version of this platform, which we are sharing with our friends and community for internal testing. We are aware that there are mistakes, gaps, and placeholders throughout the site, and we will address these in due course. Thank you for your patience and support as we continue to improve.
<p>in mixed beds with the Cask sea cucum: in mixed beds with the Cask sea cucumber
<p>in mixed beds with the Red-chested se: in mixed beds with the Red-chested sea cucumber
<p>juveniles like to hide in Smokey feat: juveniles like to hide in Smokey feather hydroid
<p>madreporite of a washed-up and dried: madreporite of a washed-up and dried specimen
<p>typical False Bay, South Coast, colou: typical False Bay, South Coast, colour morphs
<p>microCT image of adult female broodin: microCT image of adult female brooding 13 young in its brood pouches
<p>SEM image of brooded embryo extracted: SEM image of brooded embryo extracted from the broodpouch, total size 3-4 mm
<p>microCT scan - brooding adult virtual: microCT scan - brooding adult virtually opened from the oral side, brooded juveniles in brood pouches
<p>microCT scan - brooding adult with su: microCT scan - brooding adult with surface set to transparent, brooded juveniles visualized in colour
<p>microCT scan - brooded juveniles insi: microCT scan - brooded juveniles inside the adult
<p>Top left: Section through colony skel:
Top left: Section through colony skeleton showing calcareous process and contracted hydranths

Top right: Gastrozooids

Bottom: colony with processes, completely covering shell previously inhabited by a hermit crab

Scale bar units in mm.
<p>Nauplius larva under the microscope -: Nauplius larva under the microscope - magnification about 150x.
<p>Kelp blade eroded by activity of Robe: Kelp blade eroded by activity of Roberta's kelp burrower Sunamphitoe roberta
<p>De Hoop - from Bamboo kelp holdfasts: De Hoop - from Bamboo kelp holdfasts Ecklonia maxima; PhD sampling of Chaitanya Katharoyan, Kelp Holdfast Biodiversity Project, PI Dr. Nasreen Peer, NRF-FBIP Grant FBIS22101863331
<p>Dried Sour figs sold at Muizenberg ma: Dried Sour figs sold at Muizenberg market
<p><strong>Sea fans and sea whips are br: Sea fans and sea whips are branching octocorals that attach permanently to the reef, where they extend their polyps into the current to capture plankton. Their flexible colonies can grow into intricate three-dimensional structures that provide habitat for many other marine organisms.
<p><strong>Sea pens and soft corals are: Sea pens and soft corals are octocorals with fleshy, flexible colonies lacking the rigid branching skeletons of sea fans. Sea pens anchor themselves in soft sediments, while soft corals are attached to hard substrates. Both capture suspended food particles with their feathery polyps.
<p>Cladobranchs are a major group of nud: Cladobranchs are a major group of nudibranchs with finger-like, often beautifully decorated appendages (cerata) on their backs. Unlike dorids, cladobranchs have a branched digestive gland system that extends into their cerata. A more visible difference between the two major nudibranch groups is that cladobranchs do not have a circle of external gills, but rather use a variety of secondarily-derived structures for respiration. In South Africa the cladobranchs are comprised of 23 families, 53 genera and 216 species.
<p>Dorids are a major group of nudibranc: Dorids are a major group of nudibranchs with generally smooth, flattened bodies, a characteristic circle of external gills, as well as a simple, unbranched digestive system. Unlike cladobranchs, they lack dorsal appendages (cerata) on their backs.  Recent taxonomic work divides dorids into two accepted groups, the Suctoria and Porostomata, as well as a third provisional  group, sometimes referred to as ‘dirty dorids, which included the the remaining families. In South Africa, the dorids represented by 18 families, 64 genera and 323 species.
<p><strong>True jellyfish (Scyphozoa) ar: True jellyfish (Scyphozoa) are free-swimming gelatinous predators characterised by a bell-shaped body and trailing tentacles armed with stinging cells. They range from a few centimetres to over a metre in diameter and are among the most conspicuous gelatinous animals. About 15 species occur in South African waters. This section also includes the closely related box jellyfish (Cubozoa) and the superficially similar hydrozoan Aequorea, providing a practical grouping of the familiar “jellyfish” forms.
<p>Sponges are soft-bodied animals struc: Sponges are soft-bodied animals structured as cooperative cell communities that filter food from water. They are among the simplest multicellular animals in terms of body organisation, lacking a head, limbs, or distinct sensory or internal organs. Instead, they function as integrated cellular systems in which different cell types perform specialised roles within a loosely coordinated whole. This allows for a wide range of forms and sizes, often highly variable within a species, making external shape unreliable for identification. Taxonomy therefore relies mainly on microscopic examination of the internal skeleton, formed by structural elements called spicules. All sponges are filter feeders, drawing water in through small surface openings and expelling it through larger openings called oscula.
<p><strong>Amphipods are an order of sma: Amphipods are an order of small crustaceans characterised by a laterally flattened (side-to-side) body and occur in marine, freshwater and, more rarely, terrestrial environments. They are found from intertidal habitats to the deepest parts of the ocean. With over 440 species recorded in South Africa, they are highly diverse and occupy a wide range of ecological roles. About 50 species are documented here, although many more occur within the Great African Seaforest.
<p>The Brachyura (from the Greek for \u2: The Brachyura (from the Greek for “short tail”) or true crabs are decapod crustaceans characterised by a broad carapace and a short hind body folded tightly beneath the cephalothorax. Crabs with the classic crab-like body form are among the most diverse and widespread crustaceans, occurring from rocky shores and estuaries to coral reefs and the deep sea, where they occupy a wide variety of ecological roles as predators, scavengers and filter feeders. Here we cover about 25 species found in and around the Great African Seaforest.
<p>Rock lobsters are large, spiny decapo: Rock lobsters are large, spiny decapod crustaceans closely related to lobsters but lacking the large crushing claws of true lobsters. They have long antennae, a heavily armoured body and a powerful tail used for rapid backward swimming. In South Africa, rock lobsters are common on rocky reefs and kelp forests, where they are mainly nocturnal scavengers and predators and include several ecologically and commercially important species.
<p>Hermit crabs are decapod crustaceans: Hermit crabs are decapod crustaceans that protect their soft hind bodies by living inside empty gastropod shells. Unlike ‘true crabs’, the hind body (or pleon) is soft, asymmetrical and adapted to fit tightly within an empty sea snail shell. In South Africa, hermit crabs are common and diverse, with over 70 recorded species. They occur in intertidal and sandy habitats, rocky shores and kelp forests, coral reefs, and the deep sea, ranging from small cryptic species to large and conspicuous shell carriers. About 10 species occur in or around the Great African Seaforest.
<p><strong>Hydroids are usually colonial: Hydroids are usually colonial animals that grow attached to rocks, kelp, shells and other marine organisms. Resembling delicate plants or feathers, they consist of numerous tiny polyps that capture passing prey with stinging tentacles. Many species in this exceptionally diverse group also produce small jellyfish as part of their life cycle.
Klipfishes of the family Clinidae are sm: Klipfishes of the family Clinidae are small, elongate, laterally-compressed, bottom-dwelling fishes with a single dorsal fin stretching head to tail. The pelvic fin is inserted in front of the pectoral fin and consists of just 2–3 elongate rays (and one concealed spine) and the tail is rounded. All species are viviparous and the males have a prominent intromittent organ just behind the anus. The group is particularly common and diverse in intertidal and shallow water systems along the southern and western coasts of South Africa and together these make up the continent’s largest group of endemic fish species.
<p><strong>Octopods are eight-armed ceph: Octopods are eight-armed cephalopods renowned for their intelligence and remarkable camouflage. South Africa is home to around 25 species, from familiar reef-dwelling forms to poorly known deep-water species. They are curious predators capable of changing colour, texture and posture within seconds. Two species are regularly encountered in the Great African Seaforest and are featured here.
<p>Eudicot plants are flowering plants w: Eudicot plants are flowering plants with two seed leaves, net-like leaf venation, and flower parts commonly in multiples of four or five. They often have a taproot system and vascular bundles arranged in a ring within the stem. Examples include roses, sunflowers, and oak trees. They represent roughly 75% of all flowering plant (Angiosperm) species.
<p><strong>Isopods are an order of crust: Isopods are an order of crustaceans characterised by a dorsoventrally flattened (top-to-bottom) body and occur in marine, freshwater and terrestrial environments. They are found from the intertidal zone to abyssal depths and occupy a wide variety of ecological roles, from free-living scavengers and predators to parasites. With about 350 marine species recorded in South Africa, they are highly diverse and show high levels of endemism. About 35 species are documented here, although many more occur within the Great African Seaforest.
<p>The Anomura are decapod crustaceans t: The Anomura are decapod crustaceans that include hermit crabs, squat lobsters and porcelain crabs. They form a sister clade to the Brachyura (true crabs). Many species have independently evolved a crab-like appearance, with a reduced hind body. In the case of hermit crabs the hind body is adapted for living within sea shells. In South Africa, anomurans are widespread, with hermit crabs and squat lobsters being the most diverse subgroups.
<p>Shrimps and prawns are diverse decapo: Shrimps and prawns are diverse decapod crustaceans with elongated bodies and well-developed tail fans. These groups are not formal taxonomic ranks but are commonly used to describe two distinct lineages: True shrimps (Caridea, inlcluding the alpheids or ‘cracker shrimps’) typically have a more strongly curved body and brood their eggs on the pleopods beneath the abdomen. Prawns (Dendrobranchiata, including Penaeoidea) usually have a more gently curved body, different gill structure, and release their eggs directly into the water column rather than carrying them. In South Africa, both groups are diverse and widespread in coastal and offshore waters, although only a small number of species regularly occur in kelp forest habitats.
<p>The Galatheoidea combines squat lobst: The Galatheoidea combines squat lobsters and porcelain crabs, crab-like decapod crustaceans related to hermit crabs. Most species have a flattened body with long walking legs and a reduced hind body tucked beneath the thorax, although body shape varies considerably between groups. Squat lobsters are generally active crawlers associated with rocky reefs, while porcelain crabs are typically smaller, broad-bodied species that shelter beneath rocks and in crevices. In South Africa there are roughly 52 Galatheoid species, although only a few species occur in kelp forest habitats as they are mostly deep-water inhabitants.
<p><strong>Bryozoans</strong> (\u201cmos: Bryozoans (“moss animals”) are colonial filter feeders composed of hundreds to thousands of tiny individuals living within a shared skeleton. They commonly encrust rocks, kelp, shells and other marine organisms. Nearly 280 species have been recorded from South African waters. Entoprocts are superficially similar but belong to a separate evolutionary lineage. The only entoproct species (Pedicellina choanata) included here, is grouped with the bryozoans for ease of navigation.
<p><strong>Hexacorals are mostly sessile: Hexacorals are mostly sessile predators that use rings of stinging tentacles to capture prey, although many also harbour symbiotic algae. They range from colourful intertidal anemones to deep-water and reef-building corals. About 20 species are currently represented on this platform, although more than 200 species have been recorded from South African waters. The tube anemone Ceriantheopsis is a member of the separate class Ceriantharia but is included here because of its superficial similarity.
<p><strong>Siphonophores are free-swimmi: Siphonophores are free-swimming colonies composed of many specialised individuals functioning together as a single organism. Some, such as bluebottles (Physalia spp.), are familiar from beaches, while others drift through the open ocean. More than 80 species have been recorded from South African waters, some of which occasionally enter the seaforest.
Seabreams of the family Sparidae are par: Seabreams of the family Sparidae are particularly well represented in South Africa and include many important recreational and commercial fisheries species. They are laterally compressed, with a single continuous dorsal fin and forked tail. Most species are diurnal and form shoals on shallow temperate reefs, although some are territorial and/or extend to 450 m depth. Many species change sex as they grow.
<p><strong>Gobies are small, bottom-dwel: Gobies are small, bottom-dwelling fishes that are common inhabitants of coastal habitats. They are characterised by a broad, flattened head, two dorsal fins, and pelvic fins fused into a cup-like disc that allows them to cling to rocks and other surfaces. Their eyes are usually positioned high on the head, enabling them to watch for prey and predators while remaining close to the substrate. More than 100 species occur in southern Africa, although only a small number extend into the cooler waters of the Western Cape.
<p><strong>Argonauts are pelagic octopus: Argonauts are pelagic octopuses that spend their entire lives in the open ocean. Females are best known for their delicate white egg cases, or "paper nautiluses", which regularly wash ashore during the southeasterly summer winds. These are not true shells but brood chambers secreted by the dorsal arm webs. Argonauts also exhibit extreme sexual size dimorphism, with tiny, shell-less males rarely encountered. Four species are recognised globally, three of which occur in South African waters. In recent years, A. nodosus has washed ashore most frequently, despite being considered the rarest species.
<p>Monocot plants are flowering plants w: Monocot plants are flowering plants with a single embryonic leaf (cotyledon), parallel-veined leaves, and flower parts typically in multiples of three. They usually have fibrous root systems and scattered vascular bundles in their stems. Common examples include grasses, lilies, and palms. They represent roughly 25% of all flowering plant (Angiosperm) species.
<p>Two evolutionary distinct lineages co: Two evolutionary distinct lineages comprised this group of soft-bodied marine animals. Cnidarians are characterised by specialised stinging cells (cnidocytes), a simple body plan, and typically a medusa–polyp life cycle. They can occur as solitary or colonial forms and include jellyfish, Anthozoa (Hexacorallia such as anemones and hard corals, and Octocorallia such as soft corals and sea fans), hydroids, and siphonophores. Although also gelatinous, ctenophores (comb jellies) lack stinging cells and are distinguished by eight rows of ciliary bands, or “combs,” used for swimming.
<p><strong>Tanaids are an order of small: Tanaids are an order of small, shrimp-like crustaceans with elongated bodies, fused head and thoracic segments, and distinct pincer-like claws on the front legs. They occur in marine and estuarine environments from the intertidal zone to the deep sea. Some species construct burrows in soft sediments, while a few even occupy empty gastropod shells in a manner similar to hermit crabs. In South Africa, 36 species have been recorded, although the group remains poorly studied and is in need of substantial taxonomic revision.
<p>Lobsters, shrimps &amp; prawns, and s: Lobsters, shrimps & prawns, and stomatopods are diverse groups of elongated decapod and related crustaceans adapted for crawling, swimming, burrowing and active predation. Unlike crabs, most species retain an extended abdomen and tail fan, with body forms ranging from heavily armoured rock lobsters to delicate swimming shrimps and burrowing mud shrimps. Stomatopods or mantis shrimps form a separate crustacean lineage and are included here as they bear some superficial similarity at first glance.
<p>Burrowing shrimps (sand- or mudprawns: Burrowing shrimps (sand- or mudprawns) are soft-bodied decapod crustaceans adapted for a subterranean life in deep burrows. They have slender, fragile bodies and often strongly specialised claws used for digging and maintaining extensive tunnel systems in soft sediments, with some species occurring in highly specialised habitats such as sponges. In South Africa, these ghost-like crustaceans are common in sheltered sandy bays, estuaries and subtidal mudflats, where they play an important ecological role in sediment turnover, water filtration and nutrient cycling.
Feather stars are unmistakable as a grou: Feather stars are unmistakable as a group, as they sit ‘upside–down’ with the mouth facing upwards and the long, graceful arms extended up into the water column. They also attach themselves to the substratum by means of a unique ring of claw-like segmented appendages on the underside of the body. They are abundant in the fossil record dating back over 500 million years and in early forms the body was often borne on a long stalk with the cirri at its base. Such stalked forms are rare today, but the larval stages of sedentary (i.e. stalkless) species still go though a stalked developmental stage.
Octocorallia: Sea fans, sea pens & soft: Octocorallia: Sea fans, sea pens & soft corals
<p><strong>Octocorals are colonial anima: Octocorals are colonial animals whose tiny polyps each bear eight feathery tentacles. They are common inhabitants of South African reefs, with over 200 recorded species ranging from delicate sea fans and whips to soft corals and sea pens. Unlike hard corals, they do not build massive limestone reefs, although many species produce supportive skeletons of horn, calcium carbonate or microscopic calcified spicules.
<p><strong>Cuttlefishes are a group of c: Cuttlefishes are a group of cephalopods characterised by an internal calcareous cuttlebone, broad fins that run along the mantle, and extraordinary camouflage abilities. Around 30 species occur in South African waters, ranging from tiny shallow-water species to large reef and deep-sea forms. They are active predators that use rapid changes in colour, skin texture and body shape to communicate, hunt and avoid predators. Many species remain poorly known, and new discoveries continue to refine our understanding of their diversity.
<p><strong>Mysids are an order of small,: Mysids are an order of small, shrimp-like crustaceans with elongated, often transparent bodies that occur in marine, estuarine and freshwater environments. They are found in a wide range of aquatic habitats and often occur in dense swarms that form an important food source for fishes and other predators. In South Africa, 63 species have been recorded. A handful of species are documented here, although additional species are known to occur within the Great African Seaforest.
<p>Highly specialised predators with pow: Highly specialised predators with powerful raptorial claws used to strike, spear or smash prey with remarkable speed and force. Unlike true shrimps, mantis shrimps belong to a separate lineage of crustaceans known as stomatopods, characterised by a flattened body, large stalked eyes with exceptional vision, and highly modified front limbs adapted for hunting. In South Africa, 31 species have been recorded but only two of them are adapted to cold-temperate waters. One of them is sometimes seen in the kelp forest.
<p><strong>Comb jellies (Ctenophora) are: Comb jellies (Ctenophora) are gelatinous predators that swim using eight rows of beating cilia, or “combs”, which refract light into shimmering rainbow colours. Unlike actual jellyfish, they lack stinging cells and instead capture prey using sticky cells or, in some species, an expandable mouth. About 15 species have been recorded from South African waters, although a smaller number is currently represented here.
<p><strong>South African waters are home: South African waters are home to well over 100 species of squid and squid-like cephalopods. For ease of navigation, this section combines several distantly related lineages rather than representing a formal taxonomic group. They range from tiny bobtail squids to commercially important loliginids and the enigmatic Ram's horn squid. Most are active predators with eight arms and two specialised feeding tentacles, although some have evolved highly specialised body forms and lifestyles.
<p><strong>Hydrozoans are among the most: Hydrozoans are among the most diverse groups of cnidarians, including hydroids, siphonophores and many other forms. Their life cycles and body plans range from tiny branching colonies attached to the seafloor to spectacular free-swimming predators. More than 450 species have been recorded from South African waters.
<p><strong>Stalked jellyfish (Staurozoa): Stalked jellyfish (Staurozoa) are unusual, bottom-dwelling relatives of the true jellyfish. Instead of swimming freely, they attach themselves to seaweeds, rocks or other substrates by a stalk, waiting to capture passing prey with their tentacles. Although they resemble small upside-down jellyfish, their sedentary lifestyle makes them one of the most distinctive groups. Only four species have been recorded from South African waters.
<p>Copepods are a class of microscopic,: Copepods are a class of microscopic, shrimp-like crustaceans with segmented, tubular bodies and a single simple eye. They occur in nearly all marine and freshwater environments, from intertidal habitats to the deepest ocean trenches. Most species are pelagic, although benthic and parasitic forms are also common. In South Africa, 526 species have been recorded, with only a few reported here, although they are a major ecosystem driver in the Great African Seaforest.
<p>Sea angels, Sea butterflies (pteropod: Sea angels, Sea butterflies (pteropods) & Sea elephants (heteropods) are highly modified sea snails that independently adapted to a fully planktonic life in the open ocean. Most species have completely or highly reduced shells, transparent bodies, and wing-like swimming organs that allow them to actively swim rather than passively drift. As open-ocean organisms, they are only occasionally found in coastal waters or within kelp forest environments.
While snakelets and brotulas are only di: While snakelets and brotulas are only distantly related, they are grouped together here for practical reasons, as they share a somewhat similar body shape and lifestyles.
Small, fine or feathery red algae: Small, fine or feathery red algae
Soles (the related flounders), as well a:
Soles (the related flounders), as well as tonguefishes are easily recognised as a group, as they have become extremely flattened and lie on their sides, often partially buried in sediment. The eye from the lower, unpigmented, side of the body has migrated to the upper side, which is typically cryptically patterned. 

Soles lie on their left sides with both eyes placed on their right. Identification to species level is difficult, as there are about 50 regional species and colour patterns are both extremely variable within species and can quickly change in the same individual to match the background. Tonguefish lie on their right side (not left side, as in soles) with both eyes displaced onto the left side. The pectoral fin is absent and the dorsal and anal fins join seamlessly to the anal fin, so the fish comes to a point at the posterior end.
<p><strong>Cephalopods are among the mos: Cephalopods are among the most intelligent and behaviourally complex marine invertebrates. South Africa is home to around 200 species, including octopuses, cuttlefish, squid and ram's horn squid, ranging from tiny coastal species to giant deep-sea forms. They are active predators with excellent vision, rapid colour-changing abilities, and remarkable camouflage, making them some of the most fascinating inhabitants of the Great African Seaforest.

privacySettings.vimeoDescription: We use videos from the provider Vimeo (Vimeo.com, Inc., 555 West 18th Street, New York, New York 10011, USA) to provide video content. When you enable this option, a connection to Vimeo's servers will be established. This will inform the Vimeo server which of our pages you have visited. Additionally, Vimeo may store cookies on your device and process data (e.g., IP address, device information, usage behavior). Data transmission to the USA is possible. For more information about data processing by Vimeo, please see Vimeo's Privacy Policy.
privacySettings.vimeoDescriptionShort: This video is provided by Vimeo. When you enable it, a connection to Vimeo will be established and personal data (e.g., IP address) may be transmitted. By enabling, you consent to data processing by Vimeo. Vimeo's Privacy Policy.
privacySettings.rejectText: You can withdraw your consent at any time in the privacy settings.
privacySettings.youtubeDescription: We use videos from the provider YouTube (Google Ireland Limited, Gordon House, Barrow Street, Dublin 4, Ireland) to provide video content. When you enable this option, a connection to YouTube's servers will be established. This will inform the YouTube server which of our pages you have visited. Additionally, YouTube may store cookies on your device and process data (e.g., IP address, device information, usage behavior). Data transmission to the USA is possible. For more information about data processing by YouTube, please see Google's Privacy Policy.
privacySettings.youtubeDescriptionShort: This video is provided by YouTube. When you enable it, a connection to YouTube will be established and personal data (e.g., IP address) may be transmitted. By enabling, you consent to data processing by YouTube. Google's Privacy Policy.
